title: Lawfulness of processing
summary: Processing of personal data is only lawful if there is a legal basis such as consent, contract performance, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, or legitimate interests.
requirements: Must identify and document a lawful basis for each processing activity.
If relying on consent, it must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous.
If relying on legitimate interests, a Legitimate Interests Assessment (LIA) is required.
commonGaps: No documented legal basis for data processing.
Pre-ticked consent boxes used.
Bundled consent for multiple different purposes.

title: Conditions for consent
summary: Controllers must be able to demonstrate that the data subject has consented to processing. Consent must be as easy to withdraw as to give.
requirements: Maintain records of when and how consent was obtained.
Provide an easy mechanism for users to withdraw consent at any time.
Consent requests must be clearly distinguishable from other matters.
commonGaps: Making it difficult to withdraw consent.
Using pre-checked boxes.
Failing to keep records of consent.

title: Data Subject Rights
summary: Individuals have rights regarding their data: information, access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, objection, and automated decision-making.
requirements: Provide a clear Privacy Notice.
Establish processes to handle Subject Access Requests (SARs) within one month.
Implement technical mechanisms to delete or export user data upon request.
commonGaps: No process to handle account deletion requests.
Failing to respond to data requests within 30 days.
Incomplete privacy policies.

title: Data protection by design and by default
summary: Data protection must be integrated into technology and processes from the design stage. Default settings should only collect necessary data.
requirements: Implement data minimization (only collect what's needed).
Apply pseudonymization or encryption where possible.
Ensure privacy settings are set to high by default.
commonGaps: Collecting data 'just in case' it's needed later.
Defaulting user profiles to public.
Storing passwords in plain text.

title: Records of processing activities
summary: Organizations must maintain a record of processing activities (RoPA) containing details of processing purposes, data categories, recipients, and transfers.
requirements: Document all data mapping and flows.
Maintain a comprehensive RoPA document.
Include details on data retention periods and security measures.
commonGaps: No data mapping performed.
Outdated RoPA.
Failure to include third-party processors in the records.

title: Security of processing
summary: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk.
requirements: Encrypt personal data in transit and at rest.
Ensure ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability, and resilience of systems.
Regularly test and evaluate security measures.
commonGaps: Lack of encryption at rest for database backups.
No regular security testing or vulnerability scanning.
Missing access controls and least privilege implementation.

title: Personal data breaches
summary: Breaches must be reported to the supervisory authority within 72 hours. High-risk breaches must be communicated to the affected individuals without undue delay.
requirements: Establish an incident response plan.
Maintain a breach register.
Implement monitoring to detect breaches promptly.
commonGaps: No formal incident response process.
Failure to test breach response timelines.
Staff unaware of how to report a potential incident.

title: Data protection impact assessment
summary: A DPIA is required when processing is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
requirements: Conduct a DPIA for systematic evaluation, large-scale processing of special categories, or systematic monitoring.
Document risks and mitigation measures.
commonGaps: Deploying new high-risk technologies without a DPIA.
Using AI for profiling without impact assessment.

title: Data Protection Officer
summary: A DPO must be appointed if the core activities involve regular and systematic monitoring on a large scale, or large-scale processing of special categories.
requirements: Appoint a DPO if criteria are met.
Ensure the DPO has sufficient resources and independence.
Publish DPO contact details.
commonGaps: Failure to appoint a DPO when required.
Appointing a DPO with a conflict of interest (e.g., Head of Marketing).

title: Transfers to third countries
summary: Personal data transferred outside the EU/EEA must have an adequate level of protection (e.g., adequacy decision, Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules).
requirements: Identify all cross-border data transfers.
Implement Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) if transferring to a non-adequate country.
Conduct Transfer Impact Assessments (TIAs).
commonGaps: Using US-based cloud providers without proper SCCs.
No mapping of data locations and sub-processors.
